title: Kong Rate Limit Violations
spl:
index=api sourcetype="kong:access" status=429
| stats count by consumer_id, request_uri, rate_limit_plugin
| sort -count
| head 50
description: Kong `rate_limiting` plugin log lines and `429` with `RateLimit-*` headers. Identifies abusive consumers vs tight quotas.
value: Kong `rate_limiting` plugin log lines and `429` with `RateLimit-*` headers. Identifies abusive consumers vs tight quotas.
implementation: Enable plugin logging. Baseline 429s per consumer. Alert on spike vs baseline or new consumer_id hitting limit.
visualization: Bar chart (429 by consumer), Line chart (429 rate), Table (top limited routes).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Web.Web by Web.status, Web.http_method, Web.dest | sort - count
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚ÄĘ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Kong admin/access logs.
‚ÄĘ Ensure the following data sources are available: `kong:access` `status=429`, `rate_limiting` plugin log.
‚ÄĘ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Configure data collection
Enable plugin logging. Baseline 429s per consumer. Alert on spike vs baseline or new consumer_id hitting limit.

Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=api sourcetype="kong:access" status=429
| stats count by consumer_id, request_uri, rate_limit_plugin
| sort -count
| head 50
```

Understanding this SPL

**Kong Rate Limit Violations** ‚ÄĒ Kong `rate_limiting` plugin log lines and `429` with `RateLimit-*` headers. Identifies abusive consumers vs tight quotas.

Documented **Data sources**: `kong:access` `status=429`, `rate_limiting` plugin log. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Kong admin/access logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚ÄĒrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: api; **sourcetype**: kong:access. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚ÄĘ Scopes the data: index=api, sourcetype="kong:access". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚ÄĘ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by consumer_id, request_uri, rate_limit_plugin** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚ÄĘ Orders rows with `sort` ‚ÄĒ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
‚ÄĘ Limits the number of rows with `head`.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Web.Web by Web.status, Web.http_method, Web.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Kong Rate Limit Violations** ‚ÄĒ Kong `rate_limiting` plugin log lines and `429` with `RateLimit-*` headers. Identifies abusive consumers vs tight quotas.

Documented **Data sources**: `kong:access` `status=429`, `rate_limiting` plugin log. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Kong admin/access logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚ÄĒrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚ÄĘ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Web.Web` ‚ÄĒ enable acceleration for that model.
‚ÄĘ Orders rows with `sort` ‚ÄĒ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (429 by consumer), Line chart (429 rate), Table (top limited routes).
grandmaExplanation: Kong plugin log lines and with headers ‚ÄĒ so broken requests, slow pages, and errors get caught before customers complain.
